title: Integration options for private deployment — VPC peering, PrivateLink, or S2S VPN
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sam Ellis <sam.ellis@aeromed.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Integration options for private deployment — VPC peering, PrivateLink, or S2S VPN

Sam —

Thanks for the time yesterday. As we scope the private deployment for AeroMed, we want to align on the networking path you prefer so our engineering team can prepare the right templates and runbook. High-level options we're evaluating:

- VPC peering (cross-account VPC routes)
- AWS PrivateLink (endpoint services + ENIs)
- Site-to-site (S2S) VPN — IPsec tunnel to your on-prem or cloud VPC

Quick asks from our side to help decide:
1) Do you have existing Direct Connect/Transit Gateway architecture or are you primarily using S2S VPN today?
2) Any corporate policy that forbids cross-account peering or requires endpoint-protected flows (e.g., PrivateLink-only allowances)?
3) Typical peak throughput we should plan for (Gbps) and any MTU constraints?

I've attached a short comparison cheat sheet and a blank mutual action plan so we can capture the decision and next steps.

Proposed next steps: pick a primary option (we recommend PrivateLink for isolation in most cases) and schedule a 60-minute design workshop with your network/security team and our infra engineers.

Thanks,
Ben

--
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0127
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood-integration-options-cheatsheet.pdf


From: Sam Ellis <sam.ellis@aeromed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Priyanka Shah <priyanka.shah@edgeops.io>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Integration options for private deployment — VPC peering, PrivateLink, or S2S VPN

Ben —

Appreciate the summary. A few data points from our side:

- Our production VPCs today are connected to on-prem over an MPLS/DX topology; we also have a set of customer-facing cloud VPCs that still use IPsec S2S tunnels to partner networks.
- Security team preference: they like the idea of endpoint-style private connectivity (so traffic doesn't transit peered/CIDR-overlapping routes), but they also worry about operational maturity for PrivateLink across multiple accounts.
- Throughput: initial rollout is modest (300–500 Mbps sustained), but target in 12 months is ~2 Gbps for telemetry and model metrics.
- We do have a handful of RFC1918 overlaps in some older partner VPCs — that has to be considered.

I attached our current network diagram. I think we need to balance security team's preference for PrivateLink (no cross-account route table dependencies) vs. our ops team's familiarity with VPN/DX. If PrivateLink is significantly simpler to operate from your side and supports our throughput, we're open to it.

Sam

--
Sam Ellis
Head of Platform Engineering — AeroMed
sam.ellis@aeromed.com | +1 (617) 555-4098

Attachments: aeromed-current-network-diagram.png


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sam Ellis <sam.ellis@aeromed.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 01 Jul 2027 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Integration options for private deployment — VPC peering, PrivateLink, or S2S VPN

Thanks, Sam — useful details. Summarizing tradeoffs in plain terms so we can pick a path quickly: 

- VPC peering: low latency and simple routing if there are no overlapping CIDRs. But peering requires route table management and can expose larger surface area if not scoped by security groups. Not ideal if RFC1918 overlaps exist.

- PrivateLink (recommended default): keeps traffic on a provider-hosted endpoint service, avoids cross-account route propagation, and isolates the service behind ENIs in the consumer VPC. It is good for preventing lateral routes and is friendly for multi-account setups. Operational caveat: you need to provision endpoints in each consumer VPC and monitor ENI capacity.

- S2S VPN: works with legacy on-prem and partners, good stop-gap if PrivateLink cannot be adopted immediately. Can add NAT/overlap workarounds but increases management overhead and latency.

Given AeroMed's existing overlaps and the security team's preference to avoid wide peering, PrivateLink + a fallback S2S for older partner VPCs seems like the cleanest path. Karthik and Laura — can you confirm any hard engineering constraints (ENI quota, MTU, or expected failover behavior) that would push us away from PrivateLink? If not, we'll propose the design workshop and a checklist for endpoint provisioning.

Next: I'll draft a mutual action plan and include the default recommendation (PrivateLink primary, S2S fallback).

Ben

--
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Sam Ellis <sam.ellis@aeromed.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 01 Jul 2027 14:41:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Integration options for private deployment — VPC peering, PrivateLink, or S2S VPN

Ben / Sam —

Quick engineering read: we're aligned with Ben's recommendation but adding a couple of operational constraints to call out: 

- ENI & endpoint scaling: PrivateLink will create one or more ENIs in the consumer VPC per AZ. For high-throughput flows (2+ Gbps), we'll want to provision multiple endpoints across AZs and validate cross-AZ routing if the consumer uses asymmetric routing.

- MTU and encapsulation: IPsec tunnels often reduce MTU; PrivateLink preserves MTU but may still require checking downstream firewalls. Please confirm any firewall path MTU blackholes.

- IP overlap: If AeroMed has CIDR overlap with partner VPCs we cannot use peering. PrivateLink avoids the overlap issue because consumers use ENI addresses to reach the service.

- Observability: We recommend putting a small sidecar exporter on the service nodes to surface per-endpoint metrics (bytes, connections, errors) since ENI-level metrics in some clouds can be coarse.

If AeroMed security wants PrivateLink but they have a strict policy requiring provider-side TLS + mTLS, we can terminate TLS at the service and support mTLS from the consumer side. We should capture those requirements in the design workshop.

Proposed near-term plan:
1) Design workshop (60–90m) next week — collect firewall rules, ENI quotas, and MTU checks.
2) PoC: create a PrivateLink endpoint in one test VPC + a backup IPsec S2S tunnel to validate throughput and failover.
3) Production rollout plan with endpoint provisioning script and runbook.

I'll volunteer to lead the PoC with Laura.

— Karthik

--
Karthik Iyer
Principal Network Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sam Ellis <sam.ellis@aeromed.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 02 Jul 2027 15:23:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Integration options for private deployment — VPC peering, PrivateLink, or S2S VPN

Sam —

Thanks for the input and for attaching the diagram. Action items I captured below (I'll also drop these into the HubSpot deal and the Drive folder):

- Decision: PrivateLink primary; S2S VPN fallback for legacy partners (pending your security sign-off).
- Workshop: propose Tue, Jul 6, 09:00 PT — 60 minutes (Karthik + Laura + Sam + AeroMed security + Ops).
- PoC: Redwood to provision a PrivateLink endpoint in our staging account and create a test consumer endpoint in AeroMed's staging VPC. Expected completion: 2 weeks after approvals.
- Requirements to bring to the workshop: firewall egress rules, ENI quotas per AZ, target throughput for each environment, any mTLS/TLS termination requirements.

I've attached the integration cheat sheet and have linked the HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/1234567. If that all looks good, I'll send the calendar invite and a short mutual action plan doc for signature.

Ben

--
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0127
Redwood Inference

We can do the intake call next Thursday 7/8 at 14:00 PST — Sanjiv will join for the first 15 minutes for exec alignment. Please send a calendar invite with the intake agenda (SOC2 evidence matrix; data flows & network boundaries; timeline for attestation).

title: Novex MAP: responsibilities, blockers, and revised timeline
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Katherine Morales <katherine.morales@novexhealth.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-06-25T14:06:00-07:00
Subject: Novex MAP: responsibilities, blockers, and revised timeline

Hi Katherine —

Great to meet earlier. Below is a concise recap from our discovery and an initial MAP draft to align on milestones and owners. I attached Novex_MAP_v1.xlsx for easy comments. Please confirm or flag any changes.

Key decisions from call:
- Target integration scope: embeddings + session-routing for secure patient messages
- Success metric: sub-200ms median generation for core flows; cost target under $0.02/1000 tokens avg over pilot
- Data requirements: kept in VPC; audit logs required for SOC2 evidence

title: Solara MAP: capacity needs & timeline for Dedicated/Private deployment
preview:
From: Emma Roberts <emma.roberts@solarahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 25 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Solara MAP: capacity needs & timeline for Dedicated/Private deployment

Ben / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — Solara’s product and infra teams came away encouraged. We need to lock a Mutual Action Plan (MAP) that covers: evaluation milestones, procurement milestones, and the capacity reservation (Dedicated vs Private) timelines so we can align procurement and internal launch dates. A few concrete asks from our side: 

- High-level GPU pool sizing scenarios for our projected load (pilot -> 1st release -> Q4 scale) with recommended model families (H100 vs A100) and quantization options.
- Lead time ranges for Dedicated reservations vs Private/VPC/on-premstaging (how many weeks for each major milestone: provisioning, network setup, SOC2 evidence handoff, acceptance).
- A mutual timeline that pins a close window so our procurement team can commit budget.

We’d appreciate a one-page MAP and a sizing worksheet we can drop straight into procurement. Attached is an initial usage profile (pilot_telemetry.csv) — bottom-line: two models (chat 20tps peak, embedding pipeline 250 qps) and a mean sequence length of 512 tokens.

Can you get a draft MAP + capacity recommendation to us by EOD next Monday (Jun 28)? Procurement will want a clear snapshot to present to finance.

Best,
Emma Roberts
Director of ML Infrastructure
Solara Health

Attachment: pilot_telemetry.csv (csv)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Emma Roberts <emma.roberts@solarahealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Jun 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Solara MAP: capacity needs & timeline for Dedicated/Private deployment

Emma — thanks for the clarity and the telemetry file. Attached is a draft MAP (Solara_MAP_v1.xlsx) and a quick GPU sizing worksheet (gpu_pool_quickcalc.xlsx). High-level summary up front so procurement has a clear signal: 

- Recommended pilot configuration (30d): 4xH100 or 8xA100-equivalent (mixed workload: chat + embeddings), prioritized for throughput on embeddings pipeline.
- Production tranche (Q4 scale): 24xH100s (or equivalent quantized A100 pool) with autoscaling buffer for 30% headroom.
- Dedicated reservation lead time: 6–10 weeks from signed commitment to steady-state (depends on tranche scheduling).
- Private/VPC/on-prem path: 12–16 weeks for a first-pass private deployment (network + KMS + SOC2 evidence collection can extend to 18 weeks in some regions).

Proposed MAP milestones (draft):
1) Evaluation kickoff (this week) — baseline prompt sets + perf targets (owner: Solara / SRE) — target: Jun 30
2) MAP sign (contract & reservation intent) — target: Jul 7
3) Provisioning window (Dedicated tranche 1) — target start: Jul 14; steady-state by Aug 25
4) Private onboarding (if selected) — start Jul 21; staging acceptance by Sep 20
5) Production go-live (initial) — Oct 10; scale window through Nov

Next steps: I can walk procurement through the workbook on Wednesday (Jul 30) or we can do a shorter 30-minute close call this week. Karthik flagged a few technical sizing optimizations below — Marissa is tracking the MAP milestones and will own orchestration during procurement.

Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions
Redwood Inference

Attachments: Solara_MAP_v1.xlsx (xlsx), gpu_pool_quickcalc.xlsx (xlsx)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Emma Roberts <emma.roberts@solarahealth.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Jun 2027 09:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Solara MAP: capacity needs & timeline for Dedicated/Private deployment

Adding concrete sizing rationale and a couple caveats from infra: 

- Model mix & baseline: with 20 tps chat + 250 qps embeddings at seq=512, the embeddings workload dominates GPU memory and throughput. For cost-vs-latency tradeoff we recommend an H100-first approach for the latency-sensitive chat tier and quantized A100s for embeddings if Solara wants a cost-optimized pool.
- Batching & KV cache: enabling continuous batching for embeddings (grouping per-tenant vectors) can reduce required GPU count by ~25% for the same throughput; enable prefix caching for chat models to reduce end-to-end token cost.
- Private deployment notes: customer-managed KMS + private peering requires pre-flight network validation — plan a 2-week network dry run before any image push. SOC2 evidence (documentation + read-only log collection) can be started pre-provisioning to shave ~2–3 weeks off final acceptance.
- Ops handoff: recommend a 2-week SLA ramp where Redwood provides 24/7 on-call overlap for critical windows (first 14 days post go-live).

Timeline tweak (technical): if Solara signs intent by Jul 7, we can aim to have a Dedicated tranche live by Aug 18 (earlier if we prioritize allocation and do a fast-track procurement). Private onboarding still sits at ~12 weeks for full acceptance unless we parallelize network & compliance tasks (we can).

Quoted for timeline context:
> Ben's MAP draft attached — procurement wants a single slide they can present to finance.

Karthik Iyer
Head of Field Solutions
Redwood Inference

Attachment referenced: redwood_private_timeline.pdf (pdf)


From: Emma Roberts <emma.roberts@solarahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@solarahealth.com, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 01 Jul 2027 15:43:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Solara MAP: capacity needs & timeline for Dedicated/Private deployment

Ben / Karthik / Marissa — this is exactly what procurement wanted, thank you. Two quick asks before we present to finance on Monday: 

1) Can you export a one-slide summary (MAP one-pager) that calls out the committed close window and the two pricing scenarios (Dedicated reservation vs Private deployment with one-time onboarding fees)?
2) Procurement needs the SOW language and a sample reservation contract (DocuSign link preferred) so they can route to legal.

Proposed times for a short close call: Fri Jul 2 10:00-10:30 PT or Mon Jul 5 08:30-09:00 PT. We prefer Fri if possible.

If you can get the one-pager + DocuSign sample by EOD today we’ll slot procurement and finance for the Friday call.

Thanks — will loop procurement on the final MAP slide.

Emma Roberts
Director of ML Infrastructure
Solara Health

Forwarded attachments requested: Solara_MAP_onepager.pptx (pptx), Sample_Reservation_SOW.docx (docx)

title: Merge-gate operator audit trail — attestation package requested
preview:
From: Marcia Owens <marcia@shieldrisk.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: SIG Portal <sig-portal@sharedassess.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: operator audit trail for merge-gate (AtlasPay SIG)

Hi Ben,

As part of AtlasPay's SIG assessment we're validating your merge-gate controls and the operator-level audit trail for PR approvals that bypass automated gating. Specifically we need:

- A time-ordered export showing who triggered any manual merge during the last six months (username, timestamp, PR id, reason/approval reference).
- Evidence of role-based access for the merge operator (policy or role-binding).
- Keystore/credentials rotation logs demonstrating rotation events tied to the CI pipeline (if rotation impacts deploy keys).

If you can provide an attestation note and the supporting artifacts (CSV export, screenshots, short logs), that would satisfy the SIG question 3.2.1. We aim to close this control tomorrow EOD for the assessment window.

Thanks,
Marcia Owens
Senior Assessor — ShieldRisk
marcia@shieldrisk.com

P.S. If there's a recorded SOP that describes the emergency-merge authorization flow, attach that too.

2) Items you said procurement / legal will want to review: SOC 2 (scope: production runtime), data residency and retention, standard DPA language, and an indemnity/time-to-notice clause.

- Economic buyer who owns the budget for model infra (we think a VP/Head-level owner)
- Primary legal contact for vendor review (contracts + DPA)

- Send a short mutual action plan and a draft SOW + SOC2 summary by EOD tomorrow.

Hi Samir / Lena —

Thanks again for a productive discovery earlier. I pulled a short recap below and a handful of 30-minute windows to get an intro demo + light technical Q&A with your stakeholders. Goal for the session: 30min product walkthrough focused on inference performance, integration points, and a short Q&A so your SRE/infra lead can validate constraints.

Recap (1 line each):
- Need: predictable inference latency at scale for batch + user-facing flows.
- Primary KPIs: p95 latency under 200ms for 512-token requests, cost target <$0.02 / 1k tokens.
- Integrations: private VPC deployment and Azure region preference.

Proposed 30-minute demo windows (pick 1):
A) Tue Jul 6, 10:00–10:30 PT (13:00–13:30 ET) — best for product + PM focus
B) Wed Jul 7, 11:00–11:30 PT (14:00–14:30 ET) — best for tech + SRE attendees
C) Thu Jul 8, 09:00–09:30 PT (12:00–12:30 ET) — early slot, great if you want from-start-of-day engagement

Expected Redwood attendees: Ben Carter (Solutions), Monica Patel (Solutions Engineer).
Suggested NovaPlan attendees: Samir (Head of Product), Lena (Infra Lead), an SRE or cloud architect.

Quick ask: Reply with the slot that works (A/B/C) and who will attend from your side. I attached a very short mutual action plan (stub) and our product one-pager. You can also book directly via my Calendly: https://calendly.com/redwood-ben/30min-demo

Attachments: MutualActionPlan_0701.pdf (1 page stub), redwood-product-sheet.pdf (1 page)

Thanks — happy to flex if these times don't work.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
\"On our demo we typically cover: architecture, observability, rollout patterns, and cost levers.\"

title: Request: Temporary latency tolerance & conditional credit — MedLinx pilot
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Nora Patel <nora.patel@medlinx.com>, Samir Rao <samir@medlinx.com>
Cc: Procurements <procurements@medlinx.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 13:05:00 -0700
Subject: Pilot ask: temporary latency tolerance & conditional commercial concession

Hi Nora / Samir —

Thanks again for the demo last week. MedLinx is excited to move forward with the 60‑day pilot, and before we lock scheduling I wanted to surface one operational constraint on our side and propose a pragmatic path forward.

What MedLinx asked for: you mentioned in the RFP that your platform requires a strict p95 generation latency of <=200ms for inference to meet clinician workflow expectations. We’ve run synthetic loads against the model family you selected and under the expected concurrency pattern we occasionally see p95 spikes in the 300–450ms range for longer prompts.

Ask: can Redwood authorize a temporary, scoped latency tolerance (p95 <= 450ms) for the pilot for up to 90 days, paired with a reduced financial remedy footprint during that period (pro-rated service credit cap, and a small one‑time onboarding discount) so MedLinx can validate functional fit without the full latency SLO applied?

I propose:
- 90‑day pilot window starting on signature
- Latency SLO override: p95 target relaxed to 450ms for generation requests for MedLinx pilot tokens only
- Remedies during pilot: service credit capped at 5% of monthly invoice instead of standard 20% while the override is in effect
- Operational requirements: Redwood provides daily latency dashboards and an escalation contact; MedLinx agrees to a staged traffic ramp (0→50→100% over two weeks)

If this is something you can approve at the deal desk / sales leadership level, we’ll incorporate the terms into a short MOA and route for signature. If you need exec signoff, please let me know who to loop in.

Appreciate your help — we’re keen to get the pilot running quickly.

Best,
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
avery_johnson@redwood.com
+1 415‑555‑0124

Attachment: SLO-Remedy-Matrix.xlsx (table of proposed credits & caps)


From: Nora Patel <nora.patel@medlinx.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Samir Rao <samir@medlinx.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 15:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Pilot ask: temporary latency tolerance & conditional commercial concession

Avery —

Thanks for the clear proposal. We’re comfortable with a scoped pilot but Procurement and our CTO asked for two things before we sign:

1) Executive confirmation that the latency tolerance is explicitly limited to the pilot and won’t set a precedent for GA terms across our estate.
2) A contracted, capped credit schedule that gives MedLinx operational assurance (i.e., if latency exceeds agreed thresholds repeatedly, we get a predictable credit).

Can you route an exec‑level approval email + a short MOA (DocuSign) with the explicit language about pilot scope and credit caps? Samir — looping you for procurement processing.

Nora
Director, Digital Products
MedLinx Health
nora.patel@medlinx.com


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 09:22:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Pilot ask: temporary latency tolerance & conditional commercial concession

Marissa / Ben / Grace — forwarding MedLinx’s reply. They want explicit executive confirmation that the tolerance is pilot‑only and a capped credit schedule. My recommended commercial structure below — please weigh in with risks and approval authority needed.

Proposed commercial guardrails (my recommendation):
- Pilot length: 90 days
- Scope: pilot tokens/tenant only, no change to GA MSA. Clause in MOA: "This tolerance applies only to the MedLinx pilot described in Exhibit A and does not modify the main Services Agreement or any future production commitments."
- Latency tolerance: p95 <= 450ms for generation endpoints (pilot traffic only)
- Remedy schedule during pilot (capped):
  • For a single daily p95 breach: no credit (operational alert & remediation required)
  • For >4 days in a 30‑day window where daily p95 > 450ms: 2% credit of that month
  • For >10 days in a 30‑day window: 5% credit cap for that month
  • Total service credit cap while pilot active: 5% of monthly recurring charges
- One‑time onboarding discount: 7.5% applied first invoice after signature
- Auto‑reversion: at pilot end the standard SLA and remedy terms apply unless both parties agree to an amendment

Risk notes from product/ops: Grace, can you confirm that we can provide daily dashboards and the canary routing plan? Also flag any concerns about exposing the tolerance to other customers.

Ben — this is a small ARR pilot (<$75k MRR equivalent). Can Deal Desk greenlight the 7.5% onboarding discount + the capped credit schedule, or do we need exec signoff? If exec signoff needed, recommend Marissa sign with a brief explanation and we keep the duration strictly to 90 days.

Thanks — Avery

Subject: Re: Pilot ask: temporary latency tolerance & conditional commercial concession

Subject: RE: Pilot ask: temporary latency tolerance & conditional commercial concession — Executive approval (limited)

Nora / Samir —

Thank you for the clarity and for the collaborative tone. I am approving the limited concession for the MedLinx 90‑day pilot per the guardrails below. This approval is being provided on behalf of Redwood Inference Sales Leadership and is conditional on the MedLinx team signing the attached MOA and agreeing to the operational requirements.

Executive approval (conditions):
- Approval scope: one‑time, single account (MedLinx Health) pilot only; explicit MOA language will state there is no change to the post‑pilot MSA or SLA unless subsequently amended in writing and signed by both parties
- Duration: 90 calendar days from MOA signature
- Latency tolerance: p95 <= 450ms for generation endpoints (pilot traffic only)
- Remedy schedule (capped during pilot): as proposed by Avery (see attached SLO‑Remedy‑Matrix), with a total service credit cap of 5% of monthly charges while pilot active
- Commercial concession: one‑time onboarding discount of 7.5% on the first invoice
- Operational commitments: Redwood will publish daily latency dashboards to the shared drive, provide a dedicated escalation contact (ops on‑call), and implement staged traffic ramping
- Non‑precedent clause: this approval cannot be relied upon as precedent for other accounts and will be removed from the MOA upon pilot completion

Next steps: Avery, please route the MOA (DocuSign link below) and the SLO matrix. Once signed, we will kick off the onboarding canary.

Marissa Cole

Marissa_Cole@redwood.com

title: Counter-proposal: renewal notice & termination-for-convenience scope
preview:
From: Sara Lin <sara.lin@acme-corp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Delao <marcus@acme-corp.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: ACME MSA redline — auto-renew and T4C proposals

Ben — attaching our redline (ACME_MSA_Redline_v3.docx) and a short summary. Two ask areas that legal flagged: 

1) Auto-renew: we want language that makes renewal automatic for subsequent 12-month terms unless we opt-out with 60 days' written notice (current draft is 30 days).

2) Termination for convenience (T4C): we prefer T4C to be allowed at the Order Form level only (so Acme can terminate a specific service/order) and limited to a 30-day transition period. We are willing to accept a pro-rated refund for prepaid amounts.

Quick excerpt we proposed in the attached: \"Unless either party provides written notice at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Term, the Term shall automatically renew for an additional twelve (12) month period.\"

And on T4C: \"Termination for convenience by Customer shall be limited to individual Order Forms; termination shall be effective thirty (30) days after notice and Redwood will provide reasonable transition assistance.\"

Can you confirm whether Redwood can accept these changes (or propose alternative language)? Marcus and I are aiming to finalize commercial terms this week so we can push to signature.

Attachments: ACME_MSA_Redline_v3.docx (docx), Redline_Summary_ACME.pdf (pdf)

—Sara
Sara Lin
Senior Counsel, Commercial Contracts
Acme Corp


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Jun 2027 09:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: ACME MSA redline — auto-renew and T4C proposals

Looping you both for quick review. Summary of ask from Acme (see attached in original): 60-day opt-out for auto-renew + T4C limited to order form with 30-day transition and pro-rated refunds.

My initial read: 60 days is more than we prefer (30 is our standard) but tolerable if we get predictable billing alignment and can't be triggered by non-material issues. T4C across entire MSA is a no—if they want to T4C at the order form level that's more acceptable but we should negotiate a longer transition (60–90 days) and limit it to paid orders (exclude active pilots / committed purchase orders). Also want to avoid open-ended credits that could be called retroactively.

Questions for Laura: material legal risk with their proposed language? Thoughts on a 90-day transition and capped refund (pro rata) instead of an open credit?

Karthik — commercial impact: would a 60-day opt-out materially affect our capacity planning or revenue recognition? If so, can we trade off with a minimum notice + minimum term on the first order?

I suggest we propose a practical counter: 60-day renewal opt-out is OK, but: 
- renewal stays 12 months;
- termination for convenience limited to Order Forms only;
- 90-day transition for any terminated Order Form with pro-rated refunds capped at unearned prepaid fees;
- exclude pilots and pre-paid committed credits from T4C;
- require 30-day cure rights for material breach claims that lead to non-renewal (so they can't game notice).

Can you both take quick passes and I'll send a consolidated counter to Acme?

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: ACME MSA redline — auto-renew and T4C proposals

Ben — quick commercial spot: 60-day opt-out is fine from a GTM standpoint if we get one of the following in return: a) 90-day T4C transition; b) exclude pilots / trial orders from T4C; or c) a minimum first-order commitment (3 months paid). Any of those is a workable trade.

If Laura can sign off legally, I'll accept the 60-day renewal with the 90-day T4C transition and cap on refunds. Happy for you to present that back to Acme.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Director, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 11:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: ACME MSA redline — auto-renew and T4C proposals

Ben / Karthik — legal take below. I attached a redline (Legal_redline_v1.docx) with tracked edits. High-level points: 

1) Auto-renew: 60 days' notice is negotiable. We should add: (a) that the notice must be "written notice" to the other party's contracting email (avoid informal opt-outs), and (b) a carve for material breach (i.e., renewal cannot be blocked by disputes over inconsequential operational issues). Proposed language in redline: \"Either party may prevent automatic renewal by delivering written notice to the other party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Term; such notice shall not be effective during any unresolved material breach that is the subject of cure rights under Section X.\"

2) Termination for convenience: their request to limit T4C to Order Forms is acceptable as a commercial compromise but needs guardrails. Proposed legal guardrails: 
- T4C limited to Order Forms that are not designated as "Committed Orders";
- 90-day transition assistance for terminated Order Forms;
- refunds limited to unearned prepaid fees and explicitly exclude credits for downstream 3rd-party costs;
- exclude pilots/trials and any usage-based invoices pending at the time of notice.

3) Liability and mitigation: add a small cap on refunds tied to remaining term value (e.g., capped at lesser of 3 months' fees or unearned prepaid balance) to avoid unlimited exposure. Also require Customer to reasonably mitigate (i.e., stop incurring 3rd-party charges).

4) Implementation mechanics: require the parties to execute an Order Form Amendment for any T4C invocation outlining a transition plan (services, data export, handover dates). That protects both sides operationally.

If those guardrails are acceptable commercially, we can propose the consolidated language to Acme. I flagged the redline comments where we need pricing/commercial confirmation (a few items Karthik should answer).

—Laura
Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sara Lin <sara.lin@acme-corp.com>, Marcus Delao <marcus@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 15:48:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: ACME MSA redline — auto-renew and T4C proposals

Sara / Marcus — thanks for the clear redline and summary. We discussed internally and can accept a path forward that balances Acme's flexibility and Redwood's operational predictability. Proposed consolidated compromise below (we attached a short counter and the legal redline):

1) Renewal notice: 60-day written opt-out is acceptable, with the requirement that notice is sent to the designated contracting email and that renewal cannot be blocked by unresolved material-breach disputes (Laura's language attached).

2) T4C scope: We agree to permit termination for convenience at the Order Form level only, with the following guardrails: 
   - T4C does not apply to "Committed Orders" or active pilots/trials;
   - 90-day transition assistance period for any terminated Order Form;
   - Refunds limited to unearned prepaid fees and capped at the lesser of (a) unearned prepaid fees, or (b) three (3) months' fees;
   - Customer must execute an Order Form Amendment when invoking T4C that contains a transition plan.

3) Commercial trade: in exchange for the 60-day opt-out, Redwood requests either (a) exclusion of pilots from T4C, or (b) a minimum first-order commitment of 3 months for paid services. Karthik is happy to discuss which option Acme prefers.

Next steps: please review the attached counter (Redline_Summary_ACME.pdf and Legal_redline_v1.docx). If Acme is comfortable, propose a 30-minute call to finalize language — we can aim to sign the amendment by July 14 if both sides agree by next week.

From: Neha.Kapoor@redwood.com
To: mateo.alvarez@redwood.com, Karthik.Iyer@redwood.com
Cc: Sarah.Lewis@mkcorp.com
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 08:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Draft DPA and initial redlines for MKCorp — privacy counsel comments

Thanks for the ping, Mateo — reviewed MKCorp's redlines and added counsel-level comments inline. Main points below (copied to MKCorp for transparency):

1) EU transfer mechanism
- Recommendation: we accept execution of the EU SCCs (module two) and provide a signed SCC Annex rather than a separate proprietary addendum. This aligns with our standard approach and keeps execution simple for customers.
- Suggested language: we will append the most recent EC SCCs as an Annex; we will notify customers of any updates and obtain signatures where required.

2) Subprocessors (Section 4.2)
- MKCorp asks for quarterly review rights. We typically publish an up-to-date subprocessor list and notify customers 30 days in advance of material changes. Offering quarterly review sessions is operationally heavy.
- Compromise language proposed: "Redwood will publish and maintain an up-to-date list of subprocessors and provide advance notice of material additions. Upon reasonable request, Redwood will provide a summary of subprocessor categories and will coordinate a quarterly summary call with MKCorp's security team, limited to once per quarter, unless otherwise agreed."

3) Incident response and SLA credits
- We provide a 24/7 incident contact and can include an escalation contact in the DPA. Regarding SLA credits: our standard platform SLA credit structure is in the commercial schedule; we should avoid adding new credit mechanics into the DPA. Proposed response: confirm the 24/7 escalation contact and reference the existing SLA schedule for credit calculations.

4) Data localization and retention
- MKCorp inserted a clause requesting data residency guarantees in EU. We cannot guarantee physical residency without a dedicated private deployment. Propose clarifying: for hosted customers, data will be stored in the customer-designated region where available; any region-specific residency guarantees require a dedicated/private deployment and a separate addendum.

5) Audit and evidence requests
- We can provide SOC 2 Type II summary and a scoping statement; for full audit we require standard scoping/fees. Propose adding a line about providing SOC 2 report under NDA and a path for on-site audits subject to notice and cost.

Next steps I recommend:
- I will prepare an inline redline with the language above and return it to Mateo by EOD.
- Mateo, if MKCorp needs faster turnaround, offer a 30-minute review with counsel and me to walk through the compromises.

Neha Kapoor
Senior Privacy Counsel, Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: MKCorp_DPA_Redline_v1_with_privacy_comments.docx

Cc: Sarah.Lewis@mkcorp.com
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 08:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Draft DPA and initial redlines for MKCorp — privacy counsel comments

Thanks for the ping, Mateo — reviewed MKCorp's redlines and added counsel-level comments inline. Main points below (copied to MKCorp for transparency):

1) EU transfer mechanism
- Recommendation: we accept execution of the EU SCCs (module two) and provide a signed SCC Annex rather than a separate proprietary addendum. This aligns with our standard approach and keeps execution simple for customers.
- Suggested language: we will append the most recent EC SCCs as an Annex; we will notify customers of any updates and obtain signatures where required.

2) Subprocessors (Section 4.2)
- MKCorp asks for quarterly review rights. We typically publish an up-to-date subprocessor list and notify customers 30 days in advance of material changes. Offering quarterly review sessions is operationally heavy.
- Compromise language proposed: "Redwood will publish and maintain an up-to-date list of subprocessors and provide advance notice of material additions. Upon reasonable request, Redwood will provide a summary of subprocessor categories and will coordinate a quarterly summary call with MKCorp's security team, limited to once per quarter, unless otherwise agreed."

3) Incident response and SLA credits
- We provide a 24/7 incident contact and can include an escalation contact in the DPA. Regarding SLA credits: our standard platform SLA credit structure is in the commercial schedule; we should avoid adding new credit mechanics into the DPA. Proposed response: confirm the 24/7 escalation contact and reference the existing SLA schedule for credit calculations.

4) Data localization and retention
- MKCorp inserted a clause requesting data residency guarantees in EU. We cannot guarantee physical residency without a dedicated private deployment. Propose clarifying: for hosted customers, data will be stored in the customer-designated region where available; any region-specific residency guarantees require a dedicated/private deployment and a separate addendum.

5) Audit and evidence requests
- We can provide SOC 2 Type II summary and a scoping statement; for full audit we require standard scoping/fees. Propose adding a line about providing SOC 2 report under NDA and a path for on-site audits subject to notice and cost.

Next steps I recommend:
- I will prepare an inline redline with the language above and return it to Mateo by EOD.
- Mateo, if MKCorp needs faster turnaround, offer a 30-minute review with counsel and me to walk through the compromises.

Neha Kapoor
Senior Privacy Counsel, Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: MKCorp_DPA_Redline_v1_with_privacy_comments.docx

Cc: Neha.Kapoor@redwood.com, Karthik.Iyer@redwood.com

Sarah — thanks for the fast turnaround and for looping counsel. Neha's suggestions look good to us; I've attached the updated redline (minor edits to subprocessor notice and SCC language).

To keep the timeline tight: if MKCorp is comfortable with the proposed SCC annex approach and the subprocessor compromise, I'll route this for e-sign today. Proposed signing path: Redwood + MKCorp sign DPA (with SCC Annex) via DocuSign; SOC 2 report exchange to follow under NDA.

Team —

Quick note ahead of week close: Sales Ops pulled a batch of ~48 opportunities that are currently \"Negotiation/Review\" but show no owner activity in the last 60 days and have missing/placeholder close dates. These are high noise for Finance forecast; we need to triage before the Friday commit window.

- No-Go / Archive: update stage to \"Not Pursuing\" and add reason (no budget / no timeline)
- Reassign: if you are not the right owner, reassign and add a one-line note in the Opportunity comments field

Date: Wed, 9 Jun 2027 16:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call recap + MOP for next steps

Hi Elena — thanks again for taking time earlier today. Quick recap from our 30-minute call and proposed next steps so we stay aligned:

- Goal: 2-week pilot to validate latency & cost savings on your r2 workload, plus functional eval for telemetry.
- Success criteria: <10% P50 latency increase vs baseline, 25% token-cost reduction for steady-state traffic.
- Proposed scope: 3 endpoints (chat, standard generation, embeddings), sandbox dataset and 2 test prompts.
- Timeline: start week of 6/21, run 2 weeks.

I've attached the MOP (redwood-pilot-mop-v2.pdf) with responsibilities and data access checklist. Next steps I suggest:
1) You confirm internal stakeholders (who will approve the sandbox access & procurement details)
2) We agree on start date (aiming for 6/21)
3) I’ll spin up a reserved pilot environment and share credentials + dashboard.

If this looks right, could you introduce me to procurement or confirm who signs the pilot addendum?

title: Post-call sponsor briefing — root causes, success metrics, and procurement path
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Reynolds <ava.reynolds@orionhealth.com>, Tom Becker <tom.becker@orionhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call sponsor briefing — root causes, success metrics, and procurement path

Thanks again for the call this morning — quick recap while the discussion is fresh and a proposal for next steps. High-level takeaways: 

- Primary pain: slow, inconsistent inference latency on patient triage and extraction workflows (batch peaks leading to timeouts for multi-turn summaries).
- Business outcome sponsor cares about: reduce 95th percentile latency to <400ms for critical workflows and show unit cost reduction of ~30% vs current endpoint during pilot.
- Root causes we identified together: model serving contention during batch windows; lack of effective KV cache reuse across sessions; audit/compliance constraints for PHI in your current sandbox.

Decision criteria you outlined: reliability (SLO-backed), predictable unit economics, and clear compliance evidence (SOC2 + KMS strategy). Procurement path you described: procurement team review -> legal SOW and data processing addendum -> pilot PO. You mentioned the CFO review is planned in mid-July and procurement cadence targets a pilot kickoff in late July.

Proposed next steps (asks):
1) Can you confirm the decision-maker list (procurement + legal + data privacy) so we can route the right artifacts?
2) I’m attaching a short post-call summary and our proposed mutual action plan (MAP_v1.xlsx). Please scan the timeline and confirm the pilot start window.
3) We will prepare targeted evidence: SOC2 summary, KMS integration notes, and a scoped SOW for a 6-week pilot with Dedicated capacity. Please confirm if VPC-only deployment is a hard requirement for the pilot.

Attachments: redwood_postcall_summary.pdf, MAP_v1.xlsx

If that aligns, I’ll loop in our compliance lead (Marissa) to share SOC2 and data flow diagrams today. Thanks — Camila

--
Camila Reyes
Enterprise Account Exec | Redwood Inference
camila.reyes@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0132


From: Ava Reynolds <ava.reynolds@orionhealth.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@orionhealth.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 17:06:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call sponsor briefing — root causes, success metrics, and procurement path

Camila — thanks for the crisp recap. Confirming a couple clarifications from our side: 

- Priority is indeed reducing P95 latency for triage workflows and holding costs flat during pilot. The CFO emphasis is on demonstrating predictable unit economics before moving to a multi-week production ramp.
- Data residency: production data must remain within our VPC for the pilot. We recognize a dedicated/private deployment may be necessary but need to see the minimal evidence (network diagram + KMS approach).
- Procurement timeline: procurement will review vendor evidence the week of July 18; legal prefers to see a short SOW + DPA before the procurement PO.

I attached a short RFP doc (Orion_RFP_Qs.xlsx) — answers there will speed procurement. Also, Tom flagged an SLA question: can Redwood commit to a 99.9% uptime SLA for the Dedicated pilot environment?

Rough next steps from us: we’ll send a list of stakeholders (procurement/legal/privacy) by EOD tomorrow; confirm pilot budget holders.

Thanks,
Ava

Ava Reynolds
VP, Product Operations | Orion Health
ava.reynolds@orionhealth.com | (617) 555-2190

Attachment: Orion_RFP_Qs.xlsx


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Ava Reynolds <ava.reynolds@orionhealth.com>
Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@orionhealth.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 08:14:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call sponsor briefing — root causes, success metrics, and procurement path

Looping in some operational detail from infra/compliance — quick note and owner map: 

- SOC2: we can provide a one-page summary today and the SOC2 Type II attest PDF under NDA. (Attachment: SOC2_summary.pdf)
- VPC/private: we support a Private deployment (customer VPC peering) and can outline a minimal isolation plan. Marcus will own the network diagram and KMS notes.
- SLA: standard Dedicated SLA is 99.5% out of the box; 99.9% can be offered for pilots with a short contract amendment and a slightly higher reserved capacity profile (legal sign-off required).

Owners / Immediate actions:
- Marcus Lin: produce network diagram + KMS integration notes — due Thu 07/01.
- Camila: finalize MAP and proposed SOW draft to send to Ava & procurement by Thu EOD.
- Marissa: prepare SOC2 one-pager & compliance Q&A for procurement review (attached).

Escalation item: If Orion requires 99.9% SLA with liability caps unmodified, escalate to Sales Legal — that will add a 3–5 day review.

I attached the MAP_v1.xlsx and SOC2_summary.pdf for quick reference.

Marissa
Marissa Cole
Head of Customer Compliance | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai

Attachments: MAP_v1.xlsx, SOC2_summary.pdf


From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Reynolds <ava.reynolds@orionhealth.com>, Tom Becker <tom.becker@orionhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 09:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call sponsor briefing — root causes, success metrics, and procurement path

Appreciate the fast turnaround, Ava and team — thanks Marissa and Marcus for the operational owners. Final consolidation and asks: 

1) Ava — please confirm the stakeholder list (names + emails) for procurement/legal/privacy so Marissa can send the SOC2 artifact and compliance Q&A directly.
2) Confirm whether VPC-only pilot is a hard requirement. If yes, we’ll route Private deployment docs and update the SOW.
3) Confirm acceptance of the proposed pilot window starting the week of July 25 so we can reserve Dedicated capacity and produce the contract amendment for 99.9% SLA if requested.

Links: HubSpot deal RDW-ORH-0043 (https://hubspot.redwood.ai/deals/ORH-0043) and SOW draft (DocuSign link: https://app.docusign.com/some/sow/ORH-0043). I’ll send a brief version of the SOW and the MAP for signatures once we get stakeholder confirmation.

If I don’t hear back by Friday EOD (07/02), I’ll assume we need a short follow-up call with procurement next week to close the evidence loop.

Thanks — Camila

title: Post-call: HealthSync migration goals & next steps (proprietary API -> open models)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jordan Lowe <jordan@healthsync.ai>, Elsa Rivera <elsa@medmorph.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T15:02:00-07:00
Subject: Post-call: HealthSync migration goals & next steps (proprietary API -> open models)

Thanks again for the call earlier — good to meet both of you. Short recap below so we have alignment and clear next steps. Please flag anything I missed.

Quick TL;DR
- Primary objective: remove dependency on proprietary generation API (current OpenAI/Anthropic mix) while preserving or improving quality and reducing unit cost.
- Success criteria agreed: <=150ms p95 end-to-end latency (prod-like), embedding parity for semantic search (>=95% of current similarity baseline), and ~3–4x cost reduction on gen tokens for steady-state traffic.

What we heard (pain / driver summary)
- Vendor lock-in: procurement wants multi-vendor or self-host option to avoid single-provider pricing shocks.
- Cost pressure: current token spend ~1.2B tokens/mo (generation) + ~500k embeddings/mo; past quarter cost growth ~34%.
- Quality & safety: cannot regress on hallucination / safety postures — Anthropic used for higher-assurance prompts.
- Data controls & residency: EU data-at-rest requirement for certain patient flows; KMS-managed key material required.

Current stack (from the call)
- Generation: OpenAI (davinci-like) for product features, Anthropic for safety-critical assistant flows.
- Embeddings: OpenAI embeddings for search and rerank.
- Orchestration: home-grown router that abstracts model endpoints; ~120 concurrency peak during morning sync windows.

Initial model shortlist we discussed (Redwood POV)
- Llama 3 (quantized 70B variants) — good instruction-following and broad community benchmarks.
- Mistral Large / Mix — strong quality-per-parameter, promising for cost.
- Falcon-40B instruct variants — latency and open licensing fit some internal ops.
Notes: quantization + curated fine-tuning / instruction-tuning will be required to hit parity. We also mentioned the option of a hybrid routing policy (open model for bulk generation, Anthropic fallback for safety-critical paths).

Proposed pilot (two-week technical pilot + evaluation)
1) Redwood runs a controlled benchmark using a HealthSync sample prompt set (you shared a small sample on the call). We will report quality (ROUGE / embedding-sim), latency (p50/p95), and cost projections.
2) Deploy a dedicated pilot instance (isolated pool) we can spin up in your preferred cloud region; KMS integration and EU data routing configured.
3) Safety baseline: run your highest-risk prompts through Anthropic + candidate models to map divergence and failure modes.
4) Deliverables: benchmark report, recommended model + quantization profile, pilot pricing estimate, and short runbook for cutover.

Immediate asks from you
- Share a ~100–200 prompt sample and a typical embedding query set (CSV okay).
- Confirm whether EU-only data residency applies to the pilot or just production.
- Security contact for questionnaire coordination (we can pre-fill and return).

Next steps / proposed timeline
- Today:E1 — Ben to send pilot pricing estimate + Redwood pilot checklist (attached).
- By Tue 07/02: HealthSync to share sample prompts + security contact.
- Week of 07/05: Technical deep-dive with Redwood infra team (Grace + Ethan) — 60–90m.
- Two-week pilot starts within 3 business days after sample + access are provided.

Attachments: healthsync_sample_prompts.csv (placeholder), redwood_pilot_pricing_estimate.xlsx (pricing stub).

Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0142
[Attachment: redwood_pilot_pricing_estimate.xlsx (spreadsheet)]


From: Jordan Lowe <jordan@healthsync.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Elsa Rivera <elsa@medmorph.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T15:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: HealthSync migration goals & next steps (proprietary API -> open models)

Thanks Ben — nice write-up and aligned on the ask. A few clarifications from our side: 

1) Embeddings baseline: we'll send a set of ~2k representative queries and your team should report cosine similarity distribution vs our OpenAI baseline. We care most about nearest-neighbor ranking stability for our clinical note search.

2) Data residency: EU-only for PHI-adjacent flows; non-PHI can be routed to US. For the pilot we prefer EU routing for any sample data we send. KMS integration is a hard requirement for production (we will provide key rotation policy).

3) Security: attaching our security requirements matrix — can you flag gaps quickly? We need SOC2 + reasoning about ephemeral logging while the pilot runs.

4) Commercial: procurement asked for a simple SOW + pricing band for 0–2B tokens and an option for a dedicated reserved pool if the pilot looks good.

We'll share the prompt CSV and the security contact (Elsa, looping you) by Tue 07/02 as discussed.

Jordan
Jordan Lowe
Head of Integrations | HealthSync
jordan@healthsync.ai | +1 (617) 555-0123
[Attachment: healthsync_security_requirements.pdf (pdf)]

On Jun 28, 2027, at 15:02, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Thanks again for the call earlier — good to meet both of you. Short recap below so we have alignment and clear next steps. Please flag anything I missed.
> ...


From: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T16:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: HealthSync migration goals & next steps (proprietary API -> open models)

Ben — thanks for the recap. I'll own the technical deep-dive and the benchmark run. Quick notes from my side: 

- I'll ask Ethan to pre-run a small compatibility pass (embedding parity + instruction-following) against a 100-sample subset once HealthSync drops the CSV. That will let us validate toolchain latency assumptions before the full benchmark.
- For EU KMS routing: ops can provision a pilot region with customer-managed keys; I'll put together the steps and a short checklist for the security team.
- Priyom, can you draft a concise SOW that covers pilot scope + reserved-pool option? I included the template link here: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-healthsync-pilot

Will sync up with you both tomorrow AM to align on who owns what before the deep-dive.

Grace
Grace O'Connor
Platform Solutions Lead | Redwood Inference
grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com


(Attachments referenced above are stubs; actual files stored in the HealthSync pilot Drive folder.)

title: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alex Chen <alex@nimblepay.com>, Jordan Marks <jordan@nimblepay.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-27T22:02:00Z
Subject: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks

Hi Alex / Jordan —

Thanks again for taking the time today. Quick recap from our call and the specific asks we discussed so we can land on a crisp pricing anchor: 

Key items we need to confirm from your side:
- Monthly token volume range (ballpark is fine): low / mid / high scenario
- Monthly budget range you’re trying to hit (total $ per month)
- Target unit economics: any target $/1M tokens you consider an acceptable anchor?
- Workload profile: mostly short chat turns (~80–150 tokens per request) vs long RAG passes (~1–3k tokens)

High-level competitive context we referenced on the call (so we’re aligned):
- OpenAI (hosted) tends to be the baseline for quality but higher per-1M costs for GPT-family models
- Anthropic hosted models are often near-OpenAI pricing for high-quality assistant tasks
- Several open-hosts (HostX, OpenHostY) offer lower unit prices on quantized variants but may require dedicated capacity for parity in latency/throughput

Initial Redwood anchors (subject to verification once you confirm volumes):
- Hosted / pay-as-you-go: $800 per 1M tokens (recommended for rapid pilot and no commit)
- Dedicated (reserved pool, 3-month min): $500 per 1M tokens (for sustained 5M+/mo usage)
- We can do stepped discounts for 6/12-month commits (down to ~$350/1M in certain dedicated + quantized profiles)

Attachments: I’ve included a short usage_estimates.csv and a stub comparison (token-model-comparison.xlsx) — these are draft numbers until you confirm volume mix. Also attaching a one-page MA proposal (NimblePay_MAProposal_v1.pdf) so your procurement team has something to review.

Next steps I propose: you confirm the volume/budget bullets above and we’ll send a tailored price matrix (hosted vs dedicated) + a short SOW for the pilot. If a pilot looks good we can include a DocuSign link in the next email.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0124

Attachments: NimblePay_MAProposal_v1.pdf (proposal stub), usage_estimates.csv (sample monthly scenarios), token-model-comparison.xlsx (draft)

From: Alex Chen <alex@nimblepay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Marks <jordan@nimblepay.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T09:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks

Ben —

Appreciate the recap. Quick answers from our side to help you sharpen the anchor: 

Volume (current forecast):
- Low: 2M tokens / month (limited pilot)
- Mid: 6–9M tokens / month (expected after initial rollout)
- High: 15–20M tokens / month (full feature rollout across product)

Budget:
- Our internal budget band for inference is roughly $5k–$9k / month for the mid scenario; can stretch for real ROI but would need strong cost predictability

Target $/1M:
- Ideally we target <$800 per 1M tokens for hosted; if dedicated can get <$500 we’d be very interested. Our procurement team will push for a blended ~ $450–650/1M if commitments look reasonable.

Workload profile:
- Mostly short chat flows (120–200 tokens per turn) for customer support assistant (70% of requests)
- RAG for search/summaries (avg 1.2k tokens) makes up ~30% and is bursty (spikes during end-of-month reporting)

Can you break down the anchor vs OpenAI/Anthropic for the two workload buckets (chat vs RAG)? Also need confirmation on SOC2/audit evidence before we can share financial PO info.

—Alex
Alex Chen
Head of Platform, NimblePay
alex@nimblepay.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alex Chen <alex@nimblepay.com>, Jordan Marks <jordan@nimblepay.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T13:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks

Alex — thanks, that’s very helpful. Below is a condensed breakdown (chat vs RAG) with competitive context so your procurement team has something concrete to react to. Numbers are per 1M tokens and are approximate expected run-rate prices (hosted, unless otherwise noted).

Competitive snapshot (per 1M tokens) — approximations for parity model classes:
- OpenAI (high-quality assistant models): $1,600 — $2,200 / 1M (hosted)
- Anthropic (assistant-focused models): $1,200 — $1,800 / 1M (hosted)
- HostX (open-model host, quantized): $400 — $900 / 1M (hosted / limited concurrency)
- Redwood (hosted, baseline): $800 / 1M (chat-heavy workloads)
- Redwood (dedicated 3-mo commit): $500 / 1M (preferred for steady 6–15M / mo)
- Redwood (dedicated + quantized / 6–12 mo commit): down to ~$350 / 1M for long RAG-heavy profiles

Chat (short-turns, latency sensitive):
- OpenAI: $1,800 / 1M
- Anthropic: $1,350 / 1M
- Redwood hosted: $800 / 1M (lower latency SLAs available as add-on)
- Redwood dedicated: $500 / 1M (if you commit to at least 5M/mo)

RAG (longer context, heavy KV/cache usage):
- OpenAI: $2,000 / 1M
- Anthropic: $1,600 / 1M
- Redwood hosted: $900 / 1M (KV caching helps reduce effective cost)
- Redwood dedicated + quantized option: $350–450 / 1M (depending on quantization & throughput requirements)

Pilot proposal (low friction):
- 1-month pilot: 1M tokens free trial to validate quality + $700 / 1M hosted pricing for additional tokens during pilot period
- If pilot hits mid usage, we’ll provide a dedicated pricing worksheet for 3/6/12 mo commits with explicit breakpoints (we can draft the SOW).

Attachments: I’ve attached token-model-comparison.xlsx (detailed line items) and a draft SOW will follow once we confirm pilot scope. DocuSign link can be added to the SOW flow to expedite procurement.

SOC2: we can provide the SOC2_summary.pdf + an evidence bundle (read-only link) for procurement. I’ll loop in Rishi for the specific compliance ask.

If the mid scenario (~6–9M/mo) is your target, the blended ask you mentioned (~$450–650/1M) is achievable with a modest commit and a mix of dedicated instances plus caching/quantization on RAG paths.

Proposed next step: schedule a 30-min pricing review with procurement and engineering to align on the SOW and security artifacts.

Thanks —
Ben

Attachments: token-model-comparison.xlsx

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
To: Alex Chen <alex@nimblepay.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Jordan Marks <jordan@nimblepay.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T15:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks

Alex — quick note from ops: happy to run the pricing review. Small clarifications that help our SOW drafting: 

- Canary strategy: we recommend starting with Hosted for the pilot (fast onboarding) and a scheduled canary to dedicated if performance/scale looks good — this protects you from up-front overcommit.
- Fallbacks: Redwood can route RAG workloads to lower-cost quantized variants during batch windows and fall back to higher-quality models for latency-sensitive chat (helps meet your budget band).
- Calendaring: I’ve shared a 30-min slot (link below) that covers engineering + procurement availability.

Calendar: https://calendar.google.com/event?eid=pricing-review

I’ll prepare a light SOW template (SOW_NimblePay_draft.docx) and share as an editable doc before the meeting.

—Lauren
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference



From: Alex Chen <alex@nimblepay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Marks <jordan@nimblepay.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks

Thanks Ben / Lauren — this all tracks and I appreciate the transparent anchors. Two asks before we lock the pilot: 
1) Please send the SOW draft and a DocuSign-ready agreement we can circulate to procurement (we’ll need line items for pilot scope and any termination/rollback clauses).
2) Procurement needs SOC2 evidence and a pointer to where audit logs / retention controls are documented (we’re strict on log retention for financial workloads).

If you can get the SOW + SOC2_summary.pdf over today, we’ll review and confirm the 1M free-trial pilot. If that looks good, I’ll ask procurement to route a PO and we’ll sign the DocuSign.

Alex

- Our internal budget band for inference is roughly $5k–$9k / month for the mid scenario; can stretch for real ROI but would need strong cost predictability

Target $/1M:
- Ideally we target <$800 per 1M tokens for hosted; if dedicated can get <$500 we’d be very interested. Our procurement team will push for a blended ~ $450–650/1M if commitments look reasonable.

Workload profile:
- Mostly short chat flows (120–200 tokens per turn) for customer support assistant (70% of requests)
- RAG for search/summaries (avg 1.2k tokens) makes up ~30% and is bursty (spikes during end-of-month reporting)

Can you break down the anchor vs OpenAI/Anthropic for the two workload buckets (chat vs RAG)? Also need confirmation on SOC2/audit evidence before we can share financial PO info.

—Alex
Alex Chen
Head of Platform, NimblePay
alex@nimblepay.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alex Chen <alex@nimblepay.com>, Jordan Marks <jordan@nimblepay.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-28T13:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing discovery & cost anchors — token volumes and $/1M benchmarks

Alex — thanks, that’s very helpful. Below is a condensed breakdown (chat vs RAG) with competitive context so your procurement team has something concrete to react to. Numbers are per 1M tokens and are approximate expected run-rate prices (hosted, unless otherwise noted).

Competitive snapshot (per 1M tokens) — approximations for parity model classes:
- OpenAI (high-quality assistant models): $1,600 — $2,200 / 1M (hosted)
- Anthropic (assistant-focused models): $1,200 — $1,800 / 1M (hosted)
- HostX (open-model host, quantized): $400 — $900 / 1M (hosted / limited concurrency)
- Redwood (hosted, baseline): $800 / 1M (chat-heavy workloads)
- Redwood (dedicated 3-mo commit): $500 / 1M (preferred for steady 6–15M / mo)
- Redwood (dedicated + quantized / 6–12 mo commit): down to ~$350 / 1M for long RAG-heavy profiles

Chat (short-turns, latency sensitive):
- OpenAI: $1,800 / 1M
- Anthropic: $1,350 / 1M
- Redwood hosted: $800 / 1M (lower latency SLAs available as add-on)
- Redwood dedicated: $500 / 1M (if you commit to at least 5M/mo)

RAG (longer context, heavy KV/cache usage):
- OpenAI: $2,000 / 1M
- Anthropic: $1,600 / 1M
- Redwood hosted: $900 / 1M (KV caching helps reduce effective cost)
- Redwood dedicated + quantized option: $350–450 / 1M (depending on quantization & throughput requirements)

Pilot proposal (low friction):
- 1-month pilot: 1M tokens free trial to validate quality + $700 / 1M hosted pricing for additional tokens during pilot period
- If pilot hits mid usage, we’ll provide a dedicated pricing worksheet for 3/6/12 mo commits with explicit breakpoints (we can draft the SOW).

Attachments: I’ve attached token-model-comparison.xlsx (detailed line items) and a draft SOW will follow once we confirm pilot scope. DocuSign link can be added to the SOW flow to expedite procurement.

SOC2: we can provide the SOC2_summary.pdf + an evidence bundle (read-only link) for procurement. I’ll loop in Rishi for the specific compliance ask.

If the mid scenario (~6–9M/mo) is your target, the blended ask you mentioned (~$450–650/1M) is achievable with a modest commit and a mix of dedicated instances plus caching/quantization on RAG paths.

Proposed next step: schedule a 30-min pricing review with procurement and engineering to align on the SOW and security artifacts.

Thanks —
Ben

Attachments: token-model-comparison.xlsx

- Canary strategy: we recommend starting with Hosted for the pilot (fast onboarding) and a scheduled canary to dedicated if performance/scale looks good — this protects you from up-front overcommit.
- Fallbacks: Redwood can route RAG workloads to lower-cost quantized variants during batch windows and fall back to higher-quality models for latency-sensitive chat (helps meet your budget band).
- Calendaring: I’ve shared a 30-min slot (link below) that covers engineering + procurement availability.

Calendar: https://calendar.google.com/event?eid=pricing-review

title: Discrepancy: Invoice batch 042 deductions vs ledger - proposal to bridge
preview:
From: Emma Harris <emma.harris@novacloud.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Novacloud Billing <billing@novacloud.com>
Date: Mon, 21 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Hi Aditya,

Hope you’re well. We processed invoice batch 042 on 2027-05-30 and applied a standard 5% early-payment deduction to several line items. Our AR team shows the deduction was reflected on Novacloud's statement but we cannot find the corresponding credit memo or journal entry on your side to reconcile the unapplied balance.

Summary:
- Invoice batch: 042 (attached invoice_batch_042.pdf)
- Total billed: EUR 124,500
- Deductions applied by Novacloud AP: EUR 6,225 (5% on select invoices)
- Novacloud statement shows a net payment of EUR 118,275, but Redwood ledger still lists EUR 124,500 as open on several AR lines

Can you confirm whether a credit memo or AR adjustment was posted? If not, we need a journal correction or application guidance so AP can mark our account as cleared. Our month-end is approaching and we need this done by 2027-06-30.

Attaching our remittance advice and the excerpted statement. Happy to jump on a quick call if helpful.

Thanks,
Emma Harris
Senior AP Manager
Novacloud Ltd.

Attachments: invoice_batch_042.pdf (invoice list, 8 pages); novacloud_statement_may.pdf (statement excerpt)

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Harris <emma.harris@novacloud.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 21 Jun 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Hi Emma — thanks for flagging and for the attachments. Sorry for the friction here. I’ve looped in Marcus (Accounting) and Laura (Revenue Ops) to confirm ledger postings and propose an action.

Quick plan:
1) Marcus will review AR ledger entries and post any necessary credit memos or journal corrections.
2) If Redwood posted net invoices incorrectly, we’ll issue a formal credit memo and notify ProcureDesk AP to reapply the remittance.
3) We’ll confirm expected posting date and update your statement in HubSpot.

Information we need from your side to speed this: the exact remittance file (CSV) used by your bank and the AP voucher IDs for the deductions. If you can share the remittance CSV, we’ll match on trace numbers.

Marcus / Laura — assigning this to you for investigation. Laura, please also check whether this affects revenue recognition mapping for May close (we have several customers on netting terms).

I’m available 2–4pm PT today if you want a quick sync.

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: novacloud_statement_may.pdf (from Emma)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 22 Jun 2027 14:28:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Thanks for the ping — I dug into ledger. Findings below (summary):

Investigation notes:
- Posted invoices from batch 042 were entered gross (EUR 124,500) on 2027-05-30. No credit memos were created on Redwood side.
- Bank remittance shows net payment EUR 118,275. Remittance trace IDs present for invoices 042-1001 through 042-1008.
- Two invoices (042-1004 and 042-1007) had discount terms flagged; our ERP did not auto-generate credit memos because discounts were entered as negative receipts on the bank feed (they were posted to a suspense account 2405_suspense_ar).

Suggested remediation (low risk, reversible):
1) Post two credit memos for 042-1004 (EUR 1,950) and 042-1007 (EUR 4,275) matching the 5% deduction line items.
2) Move EUR 6,225 out of 2405_suspense_ar into AR offsets and apply to the open invoices.
3) Create a reversing journal for the suspense account dated 2027-06-25 with memo "Novacloud deduction reclass - batch 042".
4) Notify Novacloud AP and ProcureDesk to re-run cash application if needed.

Impact to revenue recognition: minimal — we'll book the credit memos as customer discounts and apply to the corresponding invoices. Laura, please confirm if any rev rec adjustments needed for May close; my read is this hits contra-revenue in Jun if we post dated 06/25.

I can draft the credit memos and the journal entries; need Aditya or Laura’s approval to post. If approved, I’ll schedule the posting for 2027-06-25 EOD and circulate the GL entries.

Entries I’ll prepare (preview):
- CM 042-CM-1004: Credit memo; EN: AR-042-1004; Amount EUR 1,950; Reason: early-payment deduction
- CM 042-CM-1007: Credit memo; EN: AR-042-1007; Amount EUR 4,275; Reason: early-payment deduction
- JE reversing suspense: DR 2405_suspense_ar EUR 6,225 / CR 1100_accounts_receivable EUR 6,225; Memo: Novacloud deduction reclass

Let me know — I’ll hold on posting until I get sign-off.

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Accounting Manager, Redwood Inference

Attachments: draft_cm_042.zip (contains CM PDFs and JE csv)

From: Emma Harris <emma.harris@novacloud.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: ProcureDesk AP <ap@procuredesk.com>, Novacloud Billing <billing@novacloud.com>
Date: Fri, 25 Jun 2027 11:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Thanks Marcus — that plan works for us. We’ve attached the remittance file ProcureDesk used to post the payment (remittance_advice_0625.csv). ProcureDesk AP confirmed they will re-run the cash application once you publish the credit memos and notify them.

Action items agreed:
- Marcus to post CM 042-CM-1004 and 042-CM-1007 and reverse suspense JE on 2027-06-25.
- Aditya to confirm once posted so we can confirm with ProcureDesk AP.
- Laura to advise if any revenue recognition exceptions occur for May close (Novacloud is open to adjusting close timing if needed).

Please cc me once entries are live; we expect ProcureDesk to clear the net payment within 48 hours of your posting.

Appreciate the quick turnaround.

Best regards,
Emma Harris
Senior AP Manager
Novacloud Ltd.

Attachments: remittance_advice_0625.csv (bank-level trace IDs)

- CM 042-CM-1007: Credit memo; EN: AR-042-1007; Amount EUR 4,275; Reason: early-payment deduction

- Preferred key segmentation: one key per pod vs one key per team per pod? We recommend one key per pod initially, then rotate to per-team keys after 60 days.

title: Post-demo procurement check: signatory & payment path clarification
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Evan Li <evan.li@harbortech.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 22 Jun 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo procurement check: signatory & payment path clarification

Hi Evan — thanks again for taking the demo earlier. Quick recap and three asks so we can move the pilot to procurement smoothly:

Recap: 
- Pilot scope: 3 engineers, staging integration, 30-day pilot (logs + embeddings use).
- Pricing discussed: Starter reserved capacity + per-token overage (we documented in deck).

Asks / open items: 
1) Payment path: will this be a PO, corporate card, or Marketplace purchase? If PO, any internal thresholds/timing we should expect?
2) Budget owner / finance sponsor: who signs off on the budget and who will be the final signatory for contracting?
3) Procurement contacts & docs: does procurement need SOC2 / DPA / specific ISO evidence? Any preferred PO template or DocuSign requirement?

I attached a short mutual_action_plan and a one-page procurement checklist to make it easy for your team to pass along. If it's easiest we can loop in procurement and your finance lead on a short call tomorrow.

Next steps: confirm answers to 1–3 and I'll circulate a tailored MAP and sample SOW for the pilot.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0182
Redwood Inference

Attachments: mutual_action_plan.docx (application/msword), procurement_checklist.xlsx (application/vnd.ms-excel)

From: Evan Li <evan.li@harbortech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@harbortech.com>, Sophia Zhang <sophia.zhang@harbortech.com>
Date: Tue, 22 Jun 2027 14:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo procurement check: signatory & payment path clarification

On Tue, Jun 22, 2027 at 10:05 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Evan — thanks again for taking the demo earlier. Quick recap and three asks so we can move the pilot to procurement smoothly:
> 
> Recap: 
> - Pilot scope: 3 engineers, staging integration, 30-day pilot (logs + embeddings use).
> - Pricing discussed: Starter reserved capacity + per-token overage (we documented in deck).
> 
> Asks / open items: 
> 1) Payment path: will this be a PO, corporate card, or Marketplace purchase? If PO, any internal thresholds/timing we should expect?
> 2) Budget owner / finance sponsor: who signs off on the budget and who will be the final signatory for contracting?
> 3) Procurement contacts & docs: does procurement need SOC2 / DPA / specific ISO evidence? Any preferred PO template or DocuSign requirement?

Thanks Ben — great demo. Answers inline below: 

1) Payment path: For pilot <$5k we'll likely use a corporate card. Anything above ~$50k requires a PO + finance approval. Marketplace purchase is possible (we prefer that) but it still needs procurement sign-off; it speeds procurement in some cases but adds an internal catalog review.

2) Budget owner: Sophia Zhang (Dir, Data Engineering) is the economic owner and will greenlight the budget. Final signatory is typically our VP Finance, Rajiv Menon — he signs anything >$100k, but for PO-level deals the Finance sponsor (Rajiv) needs to be looped early.

3) Procurement needs: SOC2 Type II and a standard DPA. Procurement prefers our PO template (they'll send) and DocuSign for signatures. They also often request a short IP/data flow diagram for vendor onboarding.

I'll loop in procurement and Sophia here. Procurement's point: procurement@harbortech.com (Liam Ortiz).

—Evan
Evan Li
Head of Platform Engineering | HarborTech
evan.li@harbortech.com | +1 (617) 555-0104

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Evan Li <evan.li@harbortech.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 23 Jun 2027 09:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo procurement check: signatory & payment path clarification

Thanks for the quick clarity, Evan — super helpful. Two follow-ups: 

- Sophia / Rajiv: could you confirm preferred contact email for the budget approval and the VP Finance signatory so we can route the paperwork correctly? (E.g., rajiv.menon@harbortech.com).
- Marketplace vs direct PO: if HarborTech prefers the Marketplace route, we'd like to understand any internal review windows so we can time the pilot start.

Next steps from our side: attaching the MAP and procurement checklist (same docs as before) and I'll prepare two short purchase routes for your review: (A) pilot on corporate card to get started in 1–2 weeks, (B) PO flow for pilot + scale with an estimated 3–4 week procurement lead time.

Karthik — looping you in to flag the PO vs marketplace differences from a legal/pricing perspective and to prep a short note on standard data terms (SOC2/DPA).

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
